region_of_origin: Western (widely adopted in Singapore fast-food context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers fish sandwich; the fries component is not separately coded here. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fish burger) is the closest available code; the combined set-meal with fries is not individually coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish burger with fries as a composite set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 300 g, comprising a battered fish fillet burger in a white bun with a standard side portion of salted french fries.

nutrient_source.title: Fish burger with fries — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F08020019 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Hamburger bun / white bread GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a composite fish burger with fries set meal. GI estimated at 66 based on the dominant carbohydrate components: white hamburger bun (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database) and french fries (GI ~63, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution (~55% bun, ~45% fries). Blended estimate yields GI ~66 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 66 × available carbohydrate per serving (31.97 g/100g × 300 g serving = 95.91 g) / 100 = 63.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 503 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,509 mg sodium, representing over 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, and the combination of deep-fried fries and processed bun further contributes saturated fat and refined carbohydrates that elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (31.97 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.96 g/100 g) and medium-to-high GI (~66)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this meal; the white bun and battered coating are rapidly digested, producing a high glycemic load (~63 per serving), and the low fibre content provides minimal buffering of postprandial blood glucose — portion reduction, wholegrain bun substitution, and limiting fries are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.65 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from deep-frying, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this meal; the deep-fried preparation introduces trans fats and saturated fats that raise LDL cholesterol, while the high sodium content (503 mg/100 g) elevates blood pressure risk, and the ultra-processed nature of the meal is independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fats that amplify pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise deep-fried foods such as this meal; AGEs formed during high-temperature frying and trans fats from commercial frying oils are strongly pro-inflammatory and may worsen joint symptoms — a grilled fish option is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Burger with Fries is a Western-style fast-food set meal consisting of a battered and deep-fried white fish fillet served in a soft white burger bun, typically accompanied by salted french fries. The fish fillet is coated in a seasoned batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, yielding a tender, flaky interior with a crunchy exterior. The bun is soft and lightly toasted, and the meal is commonly served with tartar sauce and shredded lettuce. Per 100 g, the meal provides 300 kcal, 7.84 g protein, 15.65 g fat, 31.97 g carbohydrate, 1.96 g dietary fibre, and 503 mg sodium. The high sodium content (503 mg/100 g) and elevated fat from deep-frying make this a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~66) and low fibre content result in a high glycemic load per serving (~63), making blood glucose management challenging. This meal is widely available at fast-food chains and casual dining outlets across Singapore and is commonly consumed as a working lunch or quick meal. It is not suitable for low-sodium, low-fat, or diabetic dietary plans without significant modification.
common_user_queries: Is fish burger with fries healthy?
How many calories in a fish burger with fries?
Can diabetics eat fish burger with fries?
Is fish burger with fries high in sodium?
Is fish burger suitable for hypertension?
Fish burger with fries glycemic index

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F08020019, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (fast-food fish sandwich) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; trans fat value of 0.3 g/100 g carried forward from input health_suitability caution note. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database components (white bun and fries). Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for GI estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for fast-food fish sandwich (USDA SR Legacy #21120) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat separately for this item; this value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 2. Trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) is carried forward from the input record's health_suitability caution note referencing commercial frying oil; this should be verified against a direct analytical source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for this composite set meal is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct entry for this specific combination as of 2026. 4. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for white hamburger bun (~71) and french fries (~63), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite meal. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical fast-food fish burger preparation in Singapore; kosher status is not confirmed. 6. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard fast-food fish burger set meal; actual weights vary by outlet. 7. Local-language names added for G6 compliance: this dish is widely consumed in Singapore and local-language names are provided for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy the Singapore-context multilingual requirement.

Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy Q1 and Q8 requirements.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document addition of local-language names.
